{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days in a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets, serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. The change is framed as infrastructure optimization but carries severe regulatory, contractual, and business continuity risks.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Retention / Compliance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery lifecycle including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated financial documents (invoices, customs declarations)." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The enforceable policy governing how long business records are stored, driven by legal/regulatory minimums — not infrastructure convenience." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory compliance." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The downstream use of delivery data by business customers to meet their own legal and regulatory obligations (tax filings, customs, audit trails)." },
      { "term": "Statute of Limitations", "definition": "The legal time window during which claims (contractual, tortious, consumer protection) can be brought. Determines the minimum period delivery records must be available as evidence." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records in the UK/EU must be retained for at least 6 years to satisfy the statute of limitations for contractual claims (6 years in England/Wales under the Limitation Act 1980; analogous periods in EU member states, some up to 10 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal constraint, not a business policy. Deleting records within the limitation period destroys evidence the company is legally required to be able to produce. No VP can override statute of limitations.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record may be auto-deleted before the applicable statute of limitations has expired for the jurisdiction in which the delivery occurred."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tax-relevant delivery records (invoices, VAT documentation, customs declarations) must be retained for a minimum of 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction (UK HMRC requires 6 years; German tax law requires 10 years; French tax law requires 6 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit within these windows. Destroying records constitutes a compliance violation that can result in penalties, estimated assessments, and criminal liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record containing tax-relevant data may be deleted before the applicable tax retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "GDPR does not mandate deletion of delivery records after 90 days. GDPR requires retention to be proportionate to the purpose — and legal compliance, tax obligations, and contractual necessity are all legitimate purposes that justify multi-year retention.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR retention periods are policy decisions based on legal basis analysis, not fixed invariants. However, the current 7-year period is well-justified by the legal obligations in BR-1 and BR-2.",
      "testable_assertion": "Data retention period has a documented legal basis under GDPR Article 6(1)(c) (legal obligation) or 6(1)(f) (legitimate interest) for each category of retained data."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customers who rely on the platform's delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have access to records for the duration of their compliance obligations. Deleting data they depend on is a breach of the platform's contractual and service obligations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If B2B customers use our data for their tax filings and audit responses, destroying it makes us the cause of their compliance failure. This is a contractual and reputational invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record used by a B2B customer for compliance purposes may be deleted before that customer's compliance retention window has closed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Consumer protection regulations (UK Consumer Rights Act 2015, EU Consumer Rights Directive) grant consumers rights to raise claims about delivered goods. Proof of delivery is essential evidence in such disputes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Destroying POD evidence while consumers can still legally dispute a delivery exposes the platform and its customers to undefendable claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "Proof of delivery records must be retained for at least the duration of the consumer dispute window applicable in the delivery jurisdiction."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer is audited by HMRC 5 years after a delivery and requests delivery records as supporting evidence for their VAT filings.", "expected_behavior": "Records must be available. Under the 90-day policy, they would have been destroyed 4+ years ago, leaving the customer unable to satisfy their audit." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer raises a claim under the Consumer Rights Act 4 years after delivery, disputing that goods were delivered to the correct address.", "expected_behavior": "The platform must produce proof of delivery. Under the 90-day policy, this evidence no longer exists, and the platform or its customer bears the loss." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border delivery involves customs declarations. EU customs authorities request documentation 3 years later.", "expected_behavior": "Customs documentation must be producible. 90-day deletion would make this impossible." },
    { "scenario": "A contractual dispute between the platform and a B2B client over SLA compliance requires historical delivery performance data.", "expected_behavior": "Delivery records serve as evidence of SLA adherence. Deleting them removes the platform's ability to defend itself in commercial disputes." },
    { "scenario": "German tax authority audits a B2B customer and requires delivery documentation going back 10 years.", "expected_behavior": "German tax law (AO §147) requires 10-year retention for certain commercial documents. The current 7-year policy may itself be insufficient for German operations." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Regulatory fines for failure to retain required records (HMRC penalties, EU tax authority estimated assessments). Exposure to undefendable legal claims where evidence has been destroyed. Potential loss of B2B customers who cannot rely on the platform for compliance. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or lost lawsuit.",
    "operational": "CATASTROPHIC for compliance functions. Legal, finance, and customer success teams lose the ability to respond to audits, disputes, and claims. B2B customer support becomes impossible for any issue older than 90 days.",
    "user": "B2B customers face direct compliance risk — they chose this platform partly because it maintained reliable delivery records. B2C consumers lose recourse for delivery disputes. Trust destruction is irreversible.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change converts a compliant system into a non-compliant one across multiple regulatory frameworks simultaneously. It is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal and contractual posture. Framing it as 'no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Cannot fulfill their own compliance obligations. Will likely churn or demand contractual guarantees the platform can no longer honor." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose ability to dispute deliveries after 90 days. Reduced consumer protection." },
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance Team", "impact": "Cannot respond to audits, regulatory inquiries, or litigation discovery requests." },
      { "group": "Finance Team", "impact": "Cannot support tax audits or VAT reconciliation beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Platform Company (as legal entity)", "impact": "Exposed to regulatory penalties, litigation losses, and potential criminal liability for document destruction." },
      { "group": "Insurance Partners", "impact": "Claims processing for lost/damaged deliveries requires historical records. 90-day deletion undermines insurance relationships." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 90,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory requirements for record retention in EU/UK logistics are well-established law, not ambiguous. The 7-year retention period aligns with standard legal practice. The risk of 90-day deletion is clear and severe. Slight uncertainty only because specific B2B contract terms and exact jurisdictional mix are not provided.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK statute of limitations requires 6-year minimum retention",
      "EU/UK tax authorities require 6-10 year retention for commercial documents",
      "GDPR does not require 90-day deletion — legal obligation is a valid retention basis",
      "B2B customers depend on this data for their own compliance",
      "This is NOT merely an infrastructure change"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact jurisdictional breakdown of deliveries (some EU states have 10-year requirements)",
      "Specific B2B contract terms regarding data availability commitments",
      "Whether any tiered approach (hot/warm/cold storage) has been considered as an alternative"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records include or are linked to tax-relevant documents (invoices, VAT records)",
      "B2B customers have contractual or implied expectations of multi-year data availability",
      "The platform has not obtained legal sign-off on the 90-day proposal"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but it fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations to B2B customers, and ability to defend against consumer claims. This is a direct contradiction — deleting legally required records is not an infrastructure optimization.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["B2B Customers", "B2C Consumers", "Legal Team", "Finance Team", "Company Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A) Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B) Implement tiered storage (hot storage for 90 days, cold/archive storage for 7+ years) to reduce costs while maintaining compliance",
        "C) Engage legal counsel for a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention schedule and delete only what is legally permissible"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Tiered storage achieves the cost reduction goal without destroying legally required records. Move records older than 90 days to cold storage (e.g., S3 Glacier, Azure Archive) at ~90% cost reduction. Option C should follow as a refinement. Option A (90-day deletion) must be rejected — it is not a viable option."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days destroys evidence required by UK/EU statutes of limitation (6+ years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days violates tax record retention requirements (6-10 years depending on jurisdiction)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days breaks contractual obligations to B2B customers who depend on this data for compliance reporting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting proof of delivery after 90 days removes the platform's ability to defend against consumer claims within the statutory dispute window", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "Characterizing this as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect and dangerous — it masks a compliance-breaking change as routine infrastructure work", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-2, BR-4, BR-5" }
  ]
}
